content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcer- PRACrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (9t6) 322-0886
March 25,2009
Robert S. Ridley
8846 East Callita Street
San Gabriel, CA 91775
Via Fax 626-744-7132
and U.S. mail
RE
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09.043
Dear Mr. Ridley:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Please note, our advice is bised on ttre facts you provide. The
Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it
provides advice. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
QIIESTIONS
1. May you accept the free travel from the Temple City Youth Development in order to
serve as a chaperone for an educational trip for 5th grade students? Will the payment be
reportable?
2. If you cannot receive the free trip, may you pay the donor the normal cost of the trip
minus any prepayments in order to comply with the gift limit of the Act?
CONCLUSIONS
l. You may accept the free travel since, under these circumstances, it is exempt from the
gift limits of the Act. However, you will need to report the fair market value as a gift from the
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government code Sections 8 I 000 through g I 01,1. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of tieCalifornia code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, untess otherwise indicated.


Our File No. 4-09-043
Page2
donor, the Temple City Youth Development. The fair market value would be the regular adult
pnce, mmus any amounts you already paid.
2. You may accept the free trip. However, you still may pay the gift down within 30
days of receipt or acceptance. [n such event the value of the gift is ieaucJa by the amount of the
reimbursement' and the amount of the any gift which must btdisclosed is reduced by the amount
of the reimbursement.
FACTS
You are an elected Board member of the Temple City Unified School District (the
"district") and Controller for the City of Pasadena. Yburcpour. is also an employee of the
district and is a board member of the Temple City Youth Development (the ..TC*D',;. Th"
TCYD is a local 50(cX3) nonprofit organization that supports eiucational opportunities for
local youth. TCYD has organized a trip for 5th grade studients from the district to washington
DC. The trip is not financially supported by the district but will be paid for by rhe students and
their families. The trip will be arranged through EF Smithsonian Tiavel, a foi profit travel
agency that offers educational tours and travel for students. Students pay $1,556, and adults pay
$1,736 for the five day trip. Under TCYD's agreement with EF Smithsonian, for every six paid
travelers, the group is allowed one free chaperone.
On March 16 and March 18,2OOg, you provided the following additional information:
o 
TCYD's board of directors consist of 12 board members and two student
teachers. TCYD's is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit corporation.
o 
{ total of 10 chaperones will be on the trip.
o 
As controller for Pasadena you are required to complete a Statement of
Economic Interests ("Form 700"), specifically schedules il, Az,c, D, E, and F.
o 
Income need only be reported if the source is located in or doing business
in the jurisdiction, is planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or f,as done
business within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time the
statement is due.
According to the Temple City Unified School District conflict of interest code Board
Members report:
l. Persons in this category shall disclose all interest in real property within the
jurisdiction.
2. Persons in this category shall disclose all investments and business positions.
3. Persons in this category shall disclose all income and business positions. ..Income,,
means a payment received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, udrun"", dividend,
interest, rent, proceeds form any sale, gift, including any gift of ftod oi beverage, loan
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received Uy tne fiter, reimbursement f; expenses, per


Our File No. 4-09-043
page 3
diem, or contribution to an insurance or pension program paid by any person other than an
employer, and including-anycommunity propertt inierestln tn.in"o*" of a spouse. lncome
also includes an outstanding loan. Income oi an individual also includes a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse o*rr, directly, indirecily
or beneficially, a l0 percent interest or greater.
ANALYSIS
The Act imposes certain obligations on public officials regarding the receipt of gifts.
o 
First, Section 99503(c) provides a gift limit of $420 in the aggregate from
a single source each calendar year for designated employees of loJJgovernment
agencies if the member or employee would be required io report the rlceipt of
income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interests.
(See, Regulation 18940.2.)
. 
sec91d, the Act requires that every local government agency adopt a
conflict-of-interest code designating categories of employees ivtro must disclose
their economic interests, including specified donors oi gift, aggregating to $50 or
more from the same source, that could foreseeably be financiitly u'ff""t.a by the
exercise of their duties. (Section 37300.) These interests are disclosed on the
Form 700.
o 
Third, Section 87100 requires that public officials disqualify themselves
from any govemmental decision that will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on a donor of gifts aggregating $420 or more in value provided
to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the
time when the decision is made. (Section g7103(e).)
You are a designated employee in two capacities, as the Controller for the City of
Pasadena and as a member of the Temple city unified school District Board. Generally, when a
designated employee receives a payment-or reimbursement for travel, that payment or
reimbursement is considered to be a gift.2 Below, we discuss the gift rules'applicable to your
travel.
Please note that although neither code specifically states that "gifts" shall be reported, the
definition of "income" for purposes of the Act includes ';any gift." (SJction g2030.) Therefore,
a gift from the sources described in the conflict of interest code would have to be reported. In
addition, while the statutory definition of "income" excludes income from sources outside the
jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the
jurisdiction, and not having done business within the jurisdiction durinlg the two years prior to
2 Section 82o28(a) provides that a gift includes any payment, including reimbursement, that confers a
personal benefit to the extent equal or greater considerationis not proviaed in return. An official who claims that a
payment is not a gift by reason ofproviding equal or greater consideration has the burden ofproving equal or greater
consideration.


Our File No. 4-09-043
Page 4
the time any statement or other action is required under this title, the statutory definition of ..gift,,
does not include such a limitation and includes gifts from sources wherever ltcated. (Holtoiay
Advice Letter, No. 4-04-043.)
Gift Limit Exception for Travel payments
Section 89503(c) provides, in pertinent part, that no designated employee of a local
elected government agency may accept gifts from any single ,o,ir". in any cuienaur y"ur *ortr,
in excess of the gift limit if the official would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts
from that source on his or her statement of economil interests. The current lift tirnit is $420.
(Regulation 18940.2.) As a designated employee of both the school district-and the city, you are
required to disclose the name and address of each source of a gift of $50 or more received during
the previous 12 months. (Section 87300; Regulation 1g730.)
Certain payments for travel, however, are not subject to the gift limit, so long as the
travel is reasonablyrelated to a legislative or gor".n-errial purposelor to an issue of state,
national, or international public policy and the travel is paidor ieimbursed by certain types of
entities, such as an organization exempt from taxation urd". Section 501(c)(i) of the lnternal
Revenue Code. This exception would apply here because the donor OCiO) is a 501(c)(3)
charitable organization and there is a governmental purpose in educaiing th" students **ough ttre
trip to Washington. Thus, under Section 89506(aX2), payment by the f-CVn 3 for transpoiation
to and from Washington and related lodging is not suuject to the git ti-it but is reportable on
your Form 700. Gifts of travel are reported on scheduie E. The value would be the cost charged
a non-chaperone adult -- $1,736, minus any prepayments or reimbursement to the donor (as
discussed below).
As described above, while the statutory definition of "income" excludes income from
sources outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do
business within the jurisdiction, and not having done business witiin the jurisdiction duririg the
two years prior to the time any statement or other action is required undei this title, the statlutory
definition of "gift" does not include such a limitation and includes gifts from sources wherever
located. (Holloway Advice Letter, supra.)
Gifts Reporting Exceptions
You have specifically inquired about paying down or reimbursing a portion of the gift.
Regulation ft9a3@)(4) states that a gifr is not considered accepted or reJeiued if:
"The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, reimburses the donor, or
the donor's ag:nt or intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift. In such event the
value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the reimbursem-ent, and the amount
3 Regulation 18945 provides that if a person makes a payment to a third party and in fact directs and
controls the use of the payment to make a gift to one or more ctearly identified orilciats, the person is the source of
the gift to the official or officials. TCYD decides who the recipienifor the free travel will ue, not EF Smithsonian
Travel, thus TCYD is the source of the gift.


Our File No. 4-09-043
page 5
of the any gift or activity expense which must be disclosed is reduced by the
amount of the reimbursement."
Thus, if you repay a portion of the value of the gift to TCYD within 30 days of receipt of
the gift, you may subtract that amount from the value o? the gift received.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
Jwwjgl
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

donor, the Temple City Youth Development. The fair market value would be the regular adult
pnce, mmus any amounts you already paid.
2. You may accept the free trip. However, you still may pay the gift down within 30
days of receipt or acceptance. [n such event the value of the gift is ieaucJa by the amount of the
reimbursement' and the amount of the any gift which must btdisclosed is reduced by the amount
of the reimbursement.
